questions.question: An artisan workshop can produce either 8 hand-woven rugs or 40 ceramic vases per month. The artisan increases rug production by 2 units. What is the opportunity cost in terms of ceramic vases?
options: 2 ceramic vases, matching the increase in rug production directly
10 ceramic vases, reflecting the trade-off ratio of vases to rugs
20 ceramic vases, representing half of the monthly vase capacity
40 ceramic vases, equal to the total ceramic vase production capacity
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost of 1 rug = 40 ÷ 8 = 5 vases. For 2 additional rugs: 2 × 5 = 10 ceramic vases sacrificed.

questions.question: Country Alpha produces a ton of steel in 10 hours and a ton of coal in 2 hours. Country Beta produces steel in 15 hours and coal in 5 hours. Which country has the absolute advantage in both goods?
options: Country Beta, because its coal and steel production times are closer together
Country Alpha, because it requires fewer labor hours for both steel and coal
Country Beta, because it produces coal with a smaller absolute time difference
Neither country, because each leads in a different product's labor time
questions.explanation: Alpha needs fewer hours for steel (10 < 15) and coal (2 < 5), giving it the absolute advantage in both goods.

questions.question: France's opportunity cost of 1 unit of wine is 2 units of cheese. Italy's opportunity cost of 1 unit of wine is 0.5 units of cheese. Which country has the comparative advantage in wine?
options: France, because it can produce more cheese in total than Italy can produce
Italy, because it sacrifices fewer units of cheese to produce each unit of wine
France, because higher opportunity cost signals greater production efficiency
Italy, because both countries produce wine at different cost structures
questions.explanation: Italy gives up only 0.5 cheese per wine vs. France's 2 cheese. The lower relative cost gives Italy the comparative advantage in wine.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, what does the slope of the Production Possibility Frontier represent?
options: The total labor supply available for allocation between the two industries
The opportunity cost of producing one good measured in units of the other good
The world relative price ratio established through international trade
The marginal rate of substitution between the two goods for all consumers
questions.explanation: The absolute value of the slope (a_LX / a_LY) indicates how much of Good Y must be given up to produce one additional unit of Good X.

questions.question: Under autarky, a country's internal price ratio is P_X/P_Y = 4, while the world price ratio is P_X/P_Y = 2. What should the country do once trade opens?
options: Export Good X because the domestic price is higher than the world price
Import Good X because it is cheaper on the world market than domestically
Export both goods because the price difference signals absolute advantage
Remain in autarky because the price difference eliminates all trade gains
questions.explanation: Good X costs 4 units of Y domestically but only 2 on the world market. The country should import X and export Y.

questions.question: In a two-country world supply model, the world relative price lies strictly between the opportunity costs of the two nations. What is the outcome?
options: Only the country with the lower opportunity cost will choose to specialize
Both countries fully specialize in their respective comparative advantage goods
Neither country specializes because the price benefits neither party fully
Production is split equally between the two goods in both trading nations
questions.explanation: When the world price is better than the domestic cost for both parties, both have a strong incentive to specialize completely.

questions.question: The labor force decreases in the country that imports Good X. How does this affect the World Relative Supply curve of Good X?
options: The entire supply curve shifts upward as production costs increase
The horizontal segment at that country's opportunity cost becomes shorter
The supply curve becomes perfectly elastic at the new world equilibrium price
The curve is unaffected because relative supply depends on prices, not labor
questions.explanation: Fewer workers reduce the nation's maximum potential output of its secondary good, shortening the flat step on the relative supply curve.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, trade opens and a country specializes fully in Good X. What happens to the real wage measured in terms of Good X?
options: It increases because trade raises the price of the exported good
It remains unchanged because the MPL in Good X is constant
It decreases as workers face competition from foreign producers
It fluctuates depending on the exchange rate between the trading nations
questions.explanation: The real wage in terms of the produced good equals the MPL, which is fixed in the Ricardian framework regardless of trade.

questions.question: Labor productivity in Country A is 4 times that of Country B, but Country A's wage is 6 times higher. Where will production occur?
options: Country A will have the cost advantage because productivity is higher
Country B will have the cost advantage because the wage premium exceeds productivity
Production will be split equally because the differences cancel each other out
Neither country has an advantage, and no trade will occur between them
questions.explanation: The wage premium (6×) exceeds the productivity premium (4×), making Country B the lower-cost producer despite lower absolute productivity.

questions.question: What is the primary source of gains from trade in the Ricardian model?
options: Increased government revenue from tariffs applied to imported goods
The ability to consume a bundle of goods that lies outside the domestic PPF
Technology transfers that occur when countries open their borders to trade
Economies of scale achieved by producing for a larger international market
questions.explanation: Trade acts like an improved technology for transforming domestic labor into foreign goods, expanding consumption beyond domestic production limits.

questions.question: In a many-good Ricardian model, why might a country's relative wage increase over time?
options: A decrease in the labor force as workers retire and exit the workforce
An increase in world demand for the specific goods the country produces
Government minimum wage legislation that raises pay across all sectors
A depreciation of the domestic currency that makes exports more competitive
questions.explanation: Higher global demand for a nation's export goods pushes up the derived demand for its labor, raising the country's relative wage level.

questions.question: A trade policy makes every single citizen in the country slightly better off than before. How is this policy categorized?
options: A potential Pareto Improvement requiring compensation to be fully realized
A strong Pareto Improvement because every individual's utility increases
A Kaldor-Hicks efficient outcome that does not require actual compensation
A utilitarian improvement that maximizes the sum of all individual utilities
questions.explanation: When every single participant's utility increases, the change qualifies as a strong Pareto Improvement without any need for compensation.

questions.question: Why does the Compensation Principle suggest trade is efficient even when some workers lose their jobs?
options: Job losses from trade are always temporary and reverse within a few months
Total gains are large enough that losers could be reimbursed while winners still gain
Governments are legally required to compensate all workers who lose jobs to trade
Workers who lose jobs from trade always find higher-paying employment elsewhere
questions.explanation: This is the essence of Kaldor-Hicks efficiency: the gains exceed the losses, so compensation is theoretically possible even if not actually paid.

questions.question: What does the Law of One Price for labor imply in the Ricardian model?
options: Wages differ permanently across sectors based on productivity differences
Wages must be equal across all sectors within a single domestic economy
International wages converge to a single global rate through free trade
The government sets a uniform wage that applies to all industries equally
questions.explanation: Perfect internal labor mobility ensures workers arbitrage away any wage differences between industries within the same country.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model, the price of Good X is \$50 and the unit labor requirement is 5 hours. What is the nominal hourly wage?
options: \$5 per hour, calculated as the unit labor requirement divided by the price
\$10 per hour, equal to the price divided by the unit labor requirement
\$50 per hour, matching the total price of one unit of Good X sold
\$250 per hour, reflecting the product of price and labor hours required
questions.explanation: Nominal wage = Price ÷ Unit Labor Requirement = \$50 ÷ 5 = \$10 per hour.

questions.question: Labor becomes less mobile between sectors in a Ricardian economy. What happens to the speed of adjustment after a world price change?
options: Adjustment speeds up because workers stay longer in their current jobs
Adjustment slows and may cause temporary unemployment during the transition
Adjustment is unaffected because prices, not mobility, drive reallocation
Adjustment becomes instantaneous as firms lower wages to retain workers
questions.explanation: Mobility is the mechanism for reallocation. Frictions and barriers to labor movement delay the realization of gains from trade.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the price of an imported good falls. What happens to the real income of workers in that import-competing sector?
options: Real income rises because cheaper imports increase their purchasing power
Real income decreases as nominal wages fall along with the product price
Real income remains stable because wages are set by long-term contracts
Real income initially drops but recovers as workers move to export industries
questions.explanation: Nominal wages fall with the product price, and because workers cannot move to export sectors, their overall purchasing power declines.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, which group is most likely to lobby actively for a protective tariff?
options: Only capital owners in the export sector who seek higher global prices
Both labor and capital owners together in the import-competing sector
Only workers in the export sector who fear foreign retaliation policies
Consumers who want the government to generate more tariff revenue
questions.explanation: Since all factors are stuck in the import-competing industry, everyone employed or invested there benefits from the price support a tariff provides.

questions.question: How does the immobile factor model contrast with the Ricardian model regarding trade's impact on income distribution?
options: Both models predict that all workers gain equally from international trade
The immobile factor model predicts clear winners and losers among labor groups
The Ricardian model shows more income inequality than the immobile factor model
Both models predict that only capital owners benefit from opening to trade
questions.explanation: The Ricardian model assumes all labor benefits from trade. The immobile factor model highlights that some labor groups can lose significantly.

questions.question: In the Heckscher-Ohlin model, a country is capital-abundant and trade opens. Which factor's real income increases?
options: Labor, because trade expands the labor-intensive domestic industries
Capital, because demand rises for the capital-intensive export good
Both factors equally, because trade benefits all factors of production
Neither factor, because factor returns are determined solely by technology
questions.explanation: Trade increases demand for the good that uses the abundant factor intensively. In a capital-abundant country, this raises the rental rate of capital.

questions.question: Country A has a Capital-to-Labor ratio of 2.0 and Country B has a ratio of 0.5. What does the H-O model predict about trade between them?
options: Both countries will export the same mix of goods because ratios are irrelevant
Country A will export the capital-intensive good while B exports the labor-intensive good
Country B will export the capital-intensive good due to its lower overall ratio
No trade will occur because the factor ratios are not identical in both countries
questions.explanation: Country A is capital-abundant (ratio 2.0 > 0.5). The H-O theorem predicts it exports capital-intensive goods and imports labor-intensive goods.

questions.question: What does the Factor Price Equalization theorem predict about international wages in the H-O framework?
options: Wage gaps between countries will widen as trade expands over time
Wages for similar labor will converge and become identical through goods trade
Only returns to capital will equalize while wage differences persist indefinitely
Factor prices are completely unrelated to trade and depend only on technology
questions.explanation: By trading goods, countries indirectly trade the factor services embodied in those goods, leading to the equalization of factor prices.

questions.question: What is the primary assumption of the H-O model that enables factor price equalization through trade?
options: One country is always much larger than the other in economic size
Both countries produce both goods and have access to identical technologies
Transportation costs are high enough to prevent complete specialization
Labor is internationally mobile while capital remains fixed within each country
questions.explanation: For factor prices to equalize mathematically, both countries must produce both goods using the same production functions. Specialization or technology gaps prevent equalization.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the price of the manufacturing good rises by 10%. By how much will the nominal wage rise?
options: By exactly 10%, matching the full increase in the manufacturing price
By less than 10%, because labor shifts lower the marginal product in manufacturing
By more than 10%, due to the Magnification Effect on factor prices
By zero percent, because wages are fixed by the price of the other good
questions.explanation: Labor moves into manufacturing, which lowers the marginal product of labor there, causing the wage increase to lag behind the price increase.

questions.question: What happens to the real return of the factor specific to the export sector after trade begins in the Specific Factor Model?
options: It decreases because foreign competition drives down domestic prices
It increases significantly as the export sector expands and gains more labor
It remains constant because specific factor returns are fixed by technology
It initially falls but recovers as workers learn new production techniques
questions.explanation: The export sector expands and attracts more mobile labor, raising the marginal productivity and thus the real return of its specific fixed factor.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, how is a Landowner (factor specific to food) affected by a rise in the price of manufactured goods?
options: They are better off because higher manufacturing prices boost the whole economy
They are definitely worse off as labor leaves and manufactured goods cost more
Their welfare is unchanged because land and manufacturing are separate sectors
They gain in nominal terms but lose in real terms, leaving the net effect zero
questions.explanation: Nominal income falls as labor departs agriculture, while the price of at least one consumption good (manufactures) rises, unambiguously reducing real welfare.

questions.question: What determines the slope and position of the labor demand curve in a sector of the Specific Factor Model?
options: The total supply of labor available to the entire domestic economy
The product of the output price and the marginal product of labor in that sector
The government-mandated minimum wage for workers in that industry
The ratio of fixed capital to fixed land across both sectors combined
questions.explanation: Firms hire until the wage equals P × MPL. The labor demand curve is the Value of the Marginal Product of Labor curve.

questions.question: Under monopolistic competition in New Trade Theory, why does trade lead to lower average production costs?
options: Foreign firms transfer their superior technology to domestic competitors
The integrated global market allows each firm to produce at higher volumes
Governments subsidize production to help domestic firms compete internationally
Trade reduces input prices as firms source cheaper raw materials from abroad
questions.explanation: Increasing returns mean that as market size and firm output grow through trade, fixed costs are spread over more units, lowering average costs.

questions.question: What is intra-industry trade in international economics?
options: Trade that occurs only between firms within the same multinational corporation
The simultaneous import and export of goods classified in the same industry
Trade in intermediate goods used as inputs in domestic production processes
The exchange of raw materials and natural resources for finished goods
questions.explanation: Intra-industry trade involves two-way exchange of similar products, such as countries both exporting and importing automobiles or electronics.

questions.question: With external economies of scale, why might a country continue importing a good it could theoretically produce more cheaply at high volumes?
options: Government trade policy prohibits domestic production of imported goods
It cannot achieve the scale needed to push costs below the established world price
Consumer preferences always favor foreign goods over domestically produced ones
International agreements prevent countries from entering new industries
questions.explanation: Without an initial large market share, the country remains stuck with high costs and cannot compete with established foreign industry clusters.

questions.question: What is the net effect of an import quota on national welfare in a small country?
options: Positive, because the quota protects domestic jobs and production capacity
Negative, due to production and consumption distortions with no offsetting gains
Neutral, because the quota's costs and benefits exactly cancel each other out
Uncertain, depending on whether the quota licenses are auctioned by the government
questions.explanation: A small country cannot improve its terms of trade. The quota creates deadweight losses from distorted production and consumption without any compensating benefit.

questions.question: What is trade creation in the context of regional trade agreements?
options: The establishment of new trade routes that did not previously exist between members
High-cost domestic production replaced by lower-cost imports from a member country
The creation of new products specifically designed for export to partner nations
Government programs that subsidize the development of export-oriented industries
questions.explanation: Trade creation is welfare-improving. It occurs when a regional agreement shifts sourcing from inefficient domestic producers to more efficient member-country producers.

questions.question: Which trade policy action is most likely to provoke retaliation from a country's trading partners?
options: A subsidy provided to domestic firms that export agricultural products
A tariff unilaterally imposed by a large country on its trading partners
A voluntary export restraint negotiated quietly through diplomatic channels
A reduction in domestic regulations that apply equally to all trading partners
questions.explanation: Large country tariffs directly harm foreign exporters, who then lobby their own governments to impose reciprocal retaliatory tariffs.

questions.question: Why might a government choose a Voluntary Export Restraint over a tariff despite the higher welfare cost?
options: VERs generate more government revenue than equivalent tariffs on imports
To avoid formal trade disputes and preserve diplomatic relations with partners
VERs are required by WTO rules while tariffs are strictly prohibited entirely
Consumers prefer VERs because they result in lower domestic prices than tariffs
questions.explanation: VERs are negotiated and labeled voluntary, helping governments avoid the political friction and potential retaliation associated with unilateral tariff increases.

questions.question: A large country imposes a tariff. How is the terms-of-trade gain from the tariff calculated?
options: The tariff rate multiplied by the total domestic consumption of the good
The reduction in the pre-tariff world price multiplied by the volume of imports
The increase in domestic producer surplus minus the loss in consumer surplus
The total government revenue collected from the tariff on all imported goods
questions.explanation: The terms-of-trade gain is the transfer from foreign exporters to the domestic economy, measured as the world price decline times the quantity imported.

questions.question: What happens to the domestic consumption of a good when a previously imposed tariff is removed?
options: Consumption decreases because domestic producers reduce their output
Consumption increases as the lower price raises the quantity demanded
Consumption remains unchanged because consumer habits are slow to adjust
Consumption initially spikes but returns to the original level within a year
questions.explanation: Removing the tariff lowers the domestic price. According to the law of demand, a lower price leads to a higher quantity consumed.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a specific tariff?
options: A 10% charge calculated on the declared value of each imported bicycle
A \$500 tax applied to every imported motorcycle regardless of its price
A variable fee that rises when import volumes exceed a threshold quantity
A tax that applies only to goods originating from certain named countries
questions.explanation: A specific tariff is a fixed dollar amount per physical unit, independent of the good's market price or assessed value.

questions.question: How does a tariff create the production distortion component of deadweight loss?
options: It forces consumers to pay higher prices for goods they previously purchased
It encourages domestic firms to produce at a marginal cost above the world price
It reduces government revenue that could have funded public infrastructure projects
It causes foreign firms to exit the market, reducing competition permanently
questions.explanation: Resources are wasted producing goods domestically at high cost that could have been purchased more cheaply on the global market.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a quota and a tariff regarding government revenue?
options: Quotas always generate more revenue than tariffs because they are more restrictive
A tariff always generates revenue, while a quota only does if licenses are auctioned
Tariffs generate revenue for foreign governments while quotas benefit domestic ones
There is no difference because both policies generate identical government revenue
questions.explanation: Unless the government auctions import licenses, the scarcity rent created by a quota typically goes to private license holders rather than the public treasury.

questions.question: What is a Local Content Requirement in international trade policy?
options: A tax on goods that contain components manufactured in foreign countries
A rule mandating that a specific percentage of a good be produced domestically
A requirement that all imported goods list their country of origin clearly
A subsidy paid to local firms for every unit of output they export abroad
questions.explanation: Local content requirements force producers to use domestic inputs or labor for a specified fraction of the product's value, boosting local industry.

questions.question: Which trade policy tool allows a lower tariff on a set quantity of imports and a higher tariff on any additional volume above that threshold?
options: A global quota that limits total imports from all countries combined
A Tariff-Rate Quota that combines a low-rate tier with a higher-rate tier
A voluntary export restraint negotiated with the principal supplying country
A countervailing duty designed to offset foreign government subsidies
questions.explanation: A TRQ is a hybrid policy providing limited market access at a low tariff rate while applying a higher tariff to imports exceeding the threshold.

questions.question: What is the definition of a Non-Tariff Barrier in international trade?
options: A tax applied only to goods that are not produced domestically in any quantity
Any policy instrument other than a tariff that restricts international trade flows
A barrier that applies exclusively to services rather than manufactured goods
A restriction that affects only imports from non-WTO member countries
questions.explanation: NTBs encompass quotas, technical standards, subsidies, licensing requirements, and administrative regulations that impede imports without using tariffs.

questions.question: In which component of the Balance of Payments are portfolio investments officially recorded?
options: The Current Account, under the services and income subcategory
The Financial Account, which tracks purchases of stocks and bonds
The Capital Account, which records all non-financial asset transfers
The Official Reserves Account, managed by the central monetary authority
questions.explanation: The Financial Account records the purchase and sale of financial assets such as stocks, bonds, and other investment instruments across borders.

questions.question: A country runs a persistent Current Account surplus. What does this indicate about its relationship with the rest of the world?
options: The country is a net borrower, accumulating debt to foreign creditors
The country is a net lender, investing its excess earnings abroad
The country has a balanced Financial Account with no net capital flows
The country is depleting its official foreign exchange reserve holdings
questions.explanation: A surplus means the country earns more from trade and investment than it spends. The excess is lent or invested abroad.

questions.question: Which of the following transactions would directly improve a country's Current Account balance?
options: A domestic firm purchasing new machinery imported from a foreign supplier
An increase in foreign tourists visiting and spending money in the country
The central bank selling foreign currency reserves to support the exchange rate
Domestic investors purchasing bonds issued by a foreign government abroad
questions.explanation: Tourism is a service export. Spending by foreign visitors counts as a credit in the Current Account, improving the overall balance.

questions.question: What does the Twin Deficits hypothesis propose about fiscal and external balances?
options: Trade deficits and budget deficits always move in opposite cyclical directions
A government budget deficit tends to cause a corresponding current account deficit
Only countries with trade surpluses can sustainably run government budget deficits
The two deficits are completely unrelated because one is fiscal and one is external
questions.explanation: If private saving and investment are stable, an increase in government borrowing must be financed by capital inflows, creating a current account deficit.

questions.question: The US inflation rate is 5% and the Japanese inflation rate is 1%. According to Purchasing Power Parity, what should happen to the Yen/Dollar exchange rate?
options: The Yen should depreciate by approximately 4% against the US Dollar
The Yen should appreciate by approximately 4% against the US Dollar
The exchange rate should remain stable because both are major currencies
The Dollar should appreciate because the US has the higher inflation rate
questions.explanation: PPP predicts the currency of the lower-inflation country (Japan at 1%) will gain value relative to the higher-inflation country (US at 5%) by roughly the inflation differential.

questions.question: What does sterilization mean when conducted by a central bank in foreign exchange markets?
options: Eliminating all foreign reserves to allow the currency to float completely freely
Offsetting the domestic money supply impact of a foreign exchange intervention
Permanently fixing the exchange rate so it can never be changed again
Banning all private foreign exchange transactions to prevent speculation
questions.explanation: For example, if the central bank buys foreign currency (injecting domestic money), it may simultaneously sell domestic bonds to absorb that liquidity.

questions.question: Under a fixed exchange rate system, a country's currency becomes overvalued. What action must the central bank take?
options: Print more domestic currency to increase its supply in global markets
Sell foreign reserves and buy domestic currency to maintain the fixed rate
Lower domestic interest rates to discourage foreign capital from entering
Allow the currency to float temporarily until the overvaluation corrects itself
questions.explanation: An overvalued currency faces downward market pressure. The central bank must buy its own currency using foreign reserves to prevent depreciation.

questions.question: What is arbitrage in the context of international financial markets?
options: Government intervention to stabilize an exchange rate at a target level
The simultaneous purchase and sale of an asset in different markets to profit from price gaps
Long-term investment in foreign assets to diversify a portfolio's risk exposure
The process of hedging against future exchange rate movements using forward contracts
questions.explanation: Arbitrage exploits price differences for the same asset across markets. This process ensures prices and exchange rates stay consistent globally.
